युक्तस्वप्नावबोधस्य योगो भवति दुःखहा ॥ १७ ॥

anvaya.meaning: of one disciplined in food and recreation
of one disciplined in effort / activity
of one disciplined in sleep and wakefulness

translations.verbatim_quote: He who is regulated in his habits of eating, sleeping, recreation and work can mitigate all material pains by practicing the yoga system.
He who is regulated in his habits of eating, sleeping, recreation and work can mitigate all material pains by practicing the yoga system.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1738 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Extravagance in the matter of eating, sleeping, defending and mating – which are demands of the body – can block advancement in the practice of yoga. As far as eating is concerned, it can be regulated only when one is practiced to take and accept prasādam, sanctified food.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.17 The ‘yoga which destroys all sorrows,’ i.e., unties bondages, is successfully practised by him who is temperate in eating and recreation, temperate in exertion, and temperate in sleep and vigil.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-17-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)

positions.rendering: regulated in his habits of eating, sleeping, recreation and work
āhāra includes mental food (cf. Ch. 7.26.2); vihāra means moving about, walking; both must be regulated
temperate in eating and recreation, in exertion, in sleep and vigil — yoga successfully practiced unties bondages

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Shankara extends āhāra to cover mental input (what one consumes, not just what one eats). Engineering analog naturally absorbs this: information diet is part of the discipline.

literal_meaning: For one disciplined in food and recreation, in actions, in sleep and wakefulness — yoga becomes the destroyer of sorrow.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 6.17 is the positive complement to 6.16. The yogi's middle-path discipline across food (āhāra), recreation (vihāra), action (ceṣṭa), sleep (svapna), and wakefulness (avabodha) makes yoga become duḥkha-hā — the destroyer of sorrow. Shankara expands āhāra to include 'mental food' (cf. Ch. 7.26.2). Ramanuja: 'the yoga which destroys all sorrows, i.e. unties bondages, is successfully practised by him who is temperate in eating and recreation, temperate in exertion, and temperate in sleep and vigil.' The verse names the concrete payoff: discipline of body produces a yoga-practice capable of dissolving suffering. Engineering analog is operational and strong.
